metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the authoritative binary yes/no question text for `respects_artifact_altitude` to the plan body (mirroring the full question text provided for `committed_work_relies_on_unbacked_claim`), so the `_BINARY_DESC` entry can be authored unambiguously.
findings.finding: respects_artifact_altitude has no defined _BINARY_DESC question wording in the plan. The plan specifies its meta-role ('graded VALIDITY input, low score DROPS the FP, FP-1') but never provides the actual binary yes/no question text the LLM verifier would receive in its _BINARY_DESC entry. An executor cannot write a conformant, unambiguous description string without independently inventing the question.
findings.location: ## What (verified code targets) — third bullet; also AC line 1 ('add their description strings to _BINARY_DESC')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan under the respects_artifact_altitude bullet: e.g. 'yes|no|insufficient|na — the finding's criticism is calibrated to the plan's own stage (plan-level artifact): the finding does not demand implementation-level proof or deployed-system evidence from a pre-merge plan.' Adjust wording to match FP-1's actual intent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py` (line 14, module docstring '7 graded questions') and `tests/unit/test_review_kernel_rules.py` (line 74, comment '7 graded sub-questions') to the edit-target list and update the hardcoded counts to '9' (or replace with a dynamic reference) when the 2 new GRADED_BINARY keys are added.
findings.finding: Two hardcoded '7 graded questions' references in `verify.py` (module docstring, line 14) and `test_review_kernel_rules.py` (comment, line 74) are not enumerated as edit targets. After adding 2 keys to GRADED_BINARY (making it 9 entries), these will silently become stale. `verify.py` line 14 is particularly visible as it is part of the public module docstring.
findings.suggested_fix: In verify.py line 14, change 'the 7 graded questions' to 'the graded questions' (drop the count, reference the tuple directly). In test_review_kernel_rules.py line 74, change 'the 7 graded sub-questions' to 'the 9 graded sub-questions' (or use `len(review_kernel.GRADED_BINARY)` in the comment).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the 'low score DROPS the FP' claim for `respects_artifact_altitude` against the generic validity() averaging arithmetic: either specify a minimum number of keys expected to score 'no' in the targeted FP-1 scenario (so the mean falls below 0.5), or acknowledge that the drop is a compound effect and update the AC5 calibration cases accordingly.
findings.finding: The decision rule 'low score DROPS the FP' for respects_artifact_altitude is not co-located with or reconciled against the GRADED_BINARY/validity() schema. The plan says respects_artifact_altitude drops FPs via low validity, but validity() averages ALL GRADED_BINARY keys equally — the plan provides no protocol for how this key's weight or semantics differs (or doesn't) from the other six, leaving the 'drops the FP' claim unresolved against the generic averaging rule.
findings.location: ## What (verified code targets) — 'respects_artifact_altitude (FP-1): graded VALIDITY input (low score DROPS the FP), not a veto'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command to AC6 — e.g., a `grep` or `ast`-based assertion that no `if field_name in (...)` or equivalent per-key branch exists in `verify.py`'s Pass-2 path — so the absence of branching is machine-checkable.
findings.finding: AC6 ('No criterion-specific branching added to Pass 2/3') is a verifiable absence claim but has no associated proving command; an executing agent cannot produce evidence that this property holds.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC6: 'No criterion-specific branching added to Pass 2/3 (the two keys are generic GRADED_BINARY entries).'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC6: 'Proof: `grep -n "committed_work_relies_on_unbacked_claim\|respects_artifact_altitude" src/rebar/llm/reviewers/verify.py` returns ONLY lines inside `GRADED_BINARY`/`_BINARY_DESC` initialisation, not inside any conditional branch; confirmed by the `test_verifier_new_subanswers` test asserting uniform loop derivation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the 'What' section how `respects_artifact_altitude` achieves its 'drops the FP' effect purely through generic validity aggregation — e.g., state explicitly that validity() already gates all low-scoring findings uniformly, so no field-specific code path is needed.
findings.finding: The 'What' section describes `respects_artifact_altitude` as a 'graded VALIDITY input (low score DROPS the FP)' implying field-specific downstream semantics, while AC6 prohibits any criterion-specific branching ('the two keys are generic GRADED_BINARY entries'). These cross-section claims are in tension: if the field truly behaves identically to every other GRADED_BINARY entry, the 'drops the FP' characterization requires an explanation of how a generic validity aggregation achieves the described FP-1 effect without special-casing.
findings.location: 'What (verified code targets)' section (respects_artifact_altitude description) vs. Acceptance Criteria — AC6
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'The "drops the FP" effect is delivered by the existing validity-gating mechanism that applies uniformly to all GRADED_BINARY fields; no per-field branching is introduced.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the canonical gloss sentence for `evidence_entails_finding` and the exact insertion point in both verifier prompts (e.g. after the existing `evidence_entails_finding` bullet), so the executor does not need to independently author the text that must satisfy the AC3 grep proof.
findings.finding: The evidence_entails_finding gloss is specified only by its grep proof target string ('already states') with no authoritative sentence to be inserted into both verifier prompts. This is an instruction-locality gap: the executor must independently author the gloss wording that produces a grep hit, and there is no canonical text to prevent divergence between the two prompt files or semantic drift from the intended meaning.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'Proof: grep -l "already states" src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_verifier*.md returns both'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan under the FP-2 gloss item: 'Insert after the evidence_entails_finding bullet in both prompts: "a finding that demands what the plan already states is not entailed — answer evidence_entails_finding=no."' This becomes the grep-proof canonical text.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the What section that `_BINARY_DESC` is a local variable inside `verification_model()` at verify.py:~125, so new description entries must be added inside that function body, not at module scope.
findings.finding: The plan describes `_BINARY_DESC` as a named entity in `review_kernel/verify.py` to which description strings should be added, but `_BINARY_DESC` is actually a LOCAL variable declared inside `verification_model()` (verify.py:125), not a module-level symbol. A developer following the plan who searches for `_BINARY_DESC` at module scope will not find it and may misplace the edit.
findings.location: ## What (verified code targets) — '(b) add their description strings to _BINARY_DESC in review_kernel/verify.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Update the plan text: '(b) add their description strings to the `_BINARY_DESC` local dict inside `verification_model()` in `review_kernel/verify.py` (at ~line 125, inside the function body)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the 'na-default RECONCILIATION' section to reflect that `validity()` in decide.py already excludes absent/na by construction (line 54); the AC2 test should be framed as a regression guard on existing behavior, not as verifying a new change.
findings.finding: The plan instructs developers to 'Confirm/adjust validity() so adding 2 keys does not retroactively penalize prior findings' and frames na-default as a reconciliation that 'must treat an ABSENT/na answer as non-answerable.' However, reading decide.py:54 shows `validity()` already does exactly this: it filters `if binary.get(q) in ('yes', 'no', 'insufficient')`, meaning absent keys and 'na' values are already excluded from the graded mean. The plan's assertion that this needs confirmation/adjustment is a false premise — no code change is required.
findings.location: ## What (verified code targets) — 'na-default RECONCILIATION: …Confirm/adjust validity() so adding 2 keys does not retroactively penalize prior findings'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Confirm/adjust validity()…' with 'validity() already excludes absent/na keys by construction (decide.py:54); AC2's unit test is a regression guard confirming this invariant is preserved after adding the 2 new GRADED_BINARY entries.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether a full ADR is warranted for this vocabulary addition, or whether a concise inline comment in `decide.py` (next to `GRADED_BINARY`) and/or an entry in `docs/review-kernel.md` suffices — citing the NOVELTY_SUBANSWERS precedent (no ADR) to justify the decision either way.
findings.finding: AC#4 mandates a new ADR for adding 2 entries to the GRADED_BINARY tuple and a gloss to two prompt files — a documentation-surface addition that no prior vocabulary expansion in this codebase warranted. NOVELTY_SUBANSWERS (a comparable vocabulary tuple introduced for the novelty sub-call) was added without an ADR. All 31 existing ADRs address runtime-behavioral, cross-cutting, or contract-level architectural decisions. An ADR for a scoped additive vocabulary extension proliferates the documentation surface without satisfying the criterion that ADRs record decisions with architectural consequence.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, item 4: 'Rationale for graded-field-vs-prompt-gloss recorded in a new ADR under docs/adr/'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the ADR requirement with a doc-comment block in `decide.py` alongside `GRADED_BINARY` explaining the graded-field-vs-prompt-gloss rationale (the same content, zero new files). If a lightweight record is still wanted, add a paragraph to the existing `docs/review-kernel.md` under a 'Vocabulary extension log' heading rather than a standalone ADR.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concrete expected validity value or inequality for the RECALL case in AC5 (e.g., 'validity score ≤ 0.4' or 'committed_work_relies_on_unbacked_claim graded as `no`, yielding validity < passing threshold X') so the test assertion is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The calibration AC (AC5) requires a test asserting that a 'well-formed ## Assumptions block with a fallback drops as low-validity', but 'low-validity' is not quantified — no threshold, range, or expected value is given — leaving the test assertion underspecified and forcing the executor to invent a criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC5: 'RECALL case (well-formed ## Assumptions block with a fallback drops as low-validity)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit work item in the 'What' section describing the ADR to be authored (scope: graded-field-vs-prompt-gloss design rationale, target path docs/adr/<name>.md) so AC4 has a corresponding decomposed task.
findings.finding: AC4 requires a new ADR under docs/adr/ with rationale for graded-field-vs-prompt-gloss, but the plan body ('Why', 'What', 'What (verified code targets)') contains no corresponding described work item for authoring this ADR — it is an orphan criterion with no decomposed task.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 'What': 'Author docs/adr/NNNN-graded-binary-vs-prompt-gloss.md capturing: (a) why committed_work_relies_on_unbacked_claim and respects_artifact_altitude are GRADED_BINARY entries rather than prompt glosses; (b) why evidence_entails_finding is a gloss rather than a binary field. This satisfies AC4.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the ## What section (or the planned ADR) an explicit positive rationale for why `committed_work_relies_on_unbacked_claim` and `respects_artifact_altitude` belong in the kernel-wide `GRADED_BINARY` rather than as criterion-local ANTI-FP guidance in finder prompts — specifically: that kernel placement ensures the verifier scores them uniformly across all criteria, enabling validity-based dropping.
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for why the two new sub-answers belong in the kernel-wide GRADED_BINARY (rather than as criterion-local rubric guidance or finder-prompt anti-FP instructions), leaving the implementer without a design justification to defend against scope creep or future refactors that might demote these to per-criterion prompts.
findings.location: ## What (verified code targets) — GRADED_BINARY expansion; ## Acceptance Criteria — AC #6
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's ## What section, add one sentence: 'Kernel-wide GRADED_BINARY membership (vs criterion-local ANTI-FP finder-prompt guidance) ensures the verifier evaluates these sub-questions for every finding regardless of criterion, so validity-based dropping (val < 0.5 → dropped) reduces the named FP/defect class uniformly — a criterion-local instruction would only affect the finder pass and would not score the verifier's own assessment.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before finalising `respects_artifact_altitude` as a new GRADED_BINARY field, provide at least one concrete example (in the plan body or ADR) where `evidence_entails_finding=yes` AND `no_viable_alternative_explanation=yes` but `respects_artifact_altitude=no`, demonstrating additive discriminating power beyond the existing two fields.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `respects_artifact_altitude` as a new GRADED_BINARY field, but the existing verifier prompts already instruct 'if a reasonable reading already satisfies the criterion, answer evidence_entails_finding=no' — the exact FP-2 case the field targets. The plan simultaneously acknowledges FP-2 is handled by a prompt *gloss* on `evidence_entails_finding` (no new key). No prior-art argument distinguishes what `respects_artifact_altitude` scores that the existing `evidence_entails_finding` + `no_viable_alternative_explanation` combination does not already cover, nor is there a Rule-of-Three example showing cases where both existing fields pass but the new one would fail.
findings.location: What (verified code targets) — 'respects_artifact_altitude (FP-1): graded VALIDITY input'; AC1 ('Both keys added to GRADED_BINARY'); plan_review_verifier.md lines 21-29 (existing charitable-reading instruction)
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section or the draft ADR, add a worked discrimination scenario: e.g. 'Finding X claims the plan lacks Y; the plan states Y in section Z; evidence_entails_finding is arguably yes (the absence claim is entailed by a selective read), no_viable_alternative_explanation is yes (no benign reading of the *absence* claim), but respects_artifact_altitude=no because the plan's own stated Y resolves the demand — score drops.' If no such scenario can be constructed, consider consolidating to the FP-2 gloss approach and dropping the field.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
